title: Anchoring Bias
summary: The first piece of information seen acts as a calibration point for all subsequent judgments. First number seen dominates price perception.
rules: Show your highest-tier option first to anchor expectations
Use a decoy option to make the target tier look reasonable
Display original price before discounted price
Feature the 'recommended' tier with visual prominence
dos: Put the most expensive plan on the left in pricing tables
Use 'Save X%' badges to reinforce the anchor comparison
donts: Don't show cheapest option first if you want to upsell
Don't present all options with equal visual weight

title: The Halo Effect (Aesthetic-Usability Effect)
summary: A single positive trait (visual beauty) biases perception of trustworthiness, competence, and security. Sloppy spacing erodes trust.
rules: Consistent spacing signals competence — use an 8px grid
Font choice signals brand personality before a word is read
First-load impression sets the entire perception of the product
Error states and edge cases still need polish — they're trust moments
dos: Use a design system or token system for spacing consistency
Polish micro-interactions — they signal attention to detail
Ensure onboarding flows look as good as marketing pages
donts: Don't launch with inconsistent spacing or misaligned elements
Don't use system-default form styles if your brand depends on trust
Don't let internal tools look broken
